Scouting Summary

Common themes

  • Right Fast, averaging 131.2 kph (up to 139.2).
  • Typical length is 7-8m (their good length); median 7.3 m.
  • Stock ball is a good length outside off, swinging in, seaming both ways, passing outside off (30% of deliveries).
  • Goes round the wicket 4% of the time against right-handers.
  • Across an over he varies his length a lot.

Biggest threats

  • Most dangerous length is Yorker/Full (5 wkts, 1.9% adjusted strike).
  • Danger pitching line: Wide of 6th.
  • Takes 17% of wickets pitching a good length on the 6th stump.
  • Beats the bat 6.4% of tracked balls (false-shot 10.6%).
  • Gets you LBW more than most — 22% of his wickets (1.4× the typical rate for this type).
  • 75% of catches are behind the wicket (keeper / slips / gully); most often to keeper, gully, slip: 3rd.

Areas to exploit

  • Concedes most runs pitching a good length on the 6th stump (10% of runs).
  • Short ball: 36% of deliveries, economy 3.4 — 10 wkts from 690 short balls.
  • Away from yorker/full, the wicket threat drops off — look to score in the less-productive lengths.
  • Most of his runs leak square/through the off side (37%) — his main scoring release.
  • Cash in when he goes full outside off (economy 4.1, beats the bat only 8%).

Solid line = career, dotted = last 3 years (where enough recent balls). Percentile within same-type peers (grey = the peer distribution, line = this bowler). Release/crease vs hand × pace/spin; movement/speed/repeatability vs pace/spin. Release & speed are modern-era (2017+ / partial). Crease variation = how much he moves his release point sideways across the crease from ball to ball (within his main angle): a high percentile = he varies where he lets the ball go a lot, a low percentile = he releases from the same spot every ball. Repeatability = length consistency over his stock-length band (2–11 m, so deliberate yorkers and bouncers don't count as poor control): a high percentile = tighter, more metronomic lengths than peers; a low percentile = he varies his length more. A marker at the very edge = he sits beyond the typical peer range on that trait.

Stock Ball & Ball Types (vs right-handers) ▶ watch stock balls

Stock ball — a good length outside off, swinging in, seaming both ways, passing outside off (30% of deliveries, economy 1.94). Minor variations: full outside off (13%) and a good length wide outside off (10%).
Ball type (pitch length × pitching line)Movement%EconWktsBeaten %
a good length outside off ▶swinging in, seaming both ways30%1.94814%
full outside off ▶swinging in, seaming both ways13%4.1428%
a good length wide outside off ▶swinging in, seaming both ways10%2.23214%
a good length in the channel ▶swinging in, seaming both ways8%2.88310%
back of a length outside off ▶seaming both ways, swinging in8%4.0733%
a good length on the stumps ▶swinging in, seaming both ways5%3.71018%
Ball type = pitch-length band × pitching-line region. Movement = swing in the air + seam/turn off the pitch, whichever is material, dominant first (ball-tracking, tracked balls only; blank if too few). Beaten % = false-shot rate on tracked balls. The stock-ball line above also notes where it passes the stumps.

Scoring Profile (vs right-handers)

54% of his runs come in boundaries — a boundary every 13 balls; most runs go to the off side (37%); the drive hurts most (53 fours/sixes at 2.6 runs/ball).

Pitch Maps & Scoring

At the Stumps (wickets)
Wicket balls pass the stumps mostly at stumps — pitches around 6th stump and moves it back.
Where They're Scored Off
Runs come mostly on the leg side (61%).
Where They Pitch It
Pitches it a good length on the 6th stump most often (15%); drops short often (36%).
Where Wickets Come From
Wickets come mostly from balls pitched a good length on the 6th stump, but strikes most often pitching wide outside off.

Speed & Spells

He is ~1.5 km/h quicker in later spells, is a touch slower in the 2nd innings, and loses ~1.9 km/h by the later days.

By spell — opening burst vs later spells.
Speed by Innings
1st vs 2nd innings of the match.
Speed by Day
By match day — fatigue across the game.

Sequencing & Over Construction

Variable with his length (length spread ±2.8 m; more repeatable than 13% of pace bowlers); pitches fuller as the over goes on; and holds the same crease position through the over.
Across 28 dismissals, his wicket ball is close in length and pace to the delivery before it — he builds pressure with repetition, not a big change-up.
Ball in overMedian lengthShort %EconWkt %

Ball 57.1 m11%3.611.6%
Ball 67.3 m12%4.141.0%
Length spread = std-dev of pitch length (smaller = more repeatable). The table shows how his length, scoring and wicket rate shift across the six balls of an over. Set-up lines compare each ball with the previous delivery in the same over (career, all batters).

Release Point & Crease Use

A low, skiddy release point (lower than 82% of right-arm pace); releases tight to the stumps (48 cm out, tighter than 94% of right-arm pace). Comes wider round the wicket (48 cm over vs 98 cm round).

Movement (vs the average pace bowler · vs right-handers)

A hit-the-deck bowler who bangs it into a hard length for steep bounce.
Generates below avg seam and below avg swing for a pace bowler; seams it in more to right-hand batters (54%).

Test-match career data. Beaten/false-shot use ~38% shot-quality tracking; turn/drift ~30% ball-tracking. Catches split by fielding position (DeliveryFielders view); some catches have no recorded position. Danger zones use empirical-Bayes shrinkage (≥3 wkts to qualify).
